items.q: Did Aquinas think conscience is the literal voice of God that cannot err?
items.correct: No - conscience is a rational faculty; it binds us even when mistaken, but it can err through faulty reasoning or ignorance.
items.misconception: Aquinas taught that conscience is God speaking directly and is therefore infallible.
items.random_wrong: Aquinas thought conscience was located in the heart muscle.
items.why: The 'voice of God' reading belongs to other thinkers; Aquinas's rational, fallible conscience is the version OCR examines.

items.q: Does emotivism mean morality is trivial and anything goes?
items.correct: No - emotivism is a theory about what moral language does, not a licence; emotivists can hold passionate moral commitments and argue about the facts behind them.
items.misconception: If emotivism is true, no one can object to any behaviour.
items.random_wrong: Emotivism is the study of emotions in animals.
items.why: Confusing a semantic theory with moral nihilism short-circuits evaluation of non-cognitivism.

items.q: Does Freud's super-ego theory settle whether conscience is reliable?
items.correct: Not by itself - explaining conscience's psychological origins does not show its judgements are wrong, though it does challenge views that treat conscience as a direct divine voice.
items.misconception: Freud proved that conscience is meaningless and should be ignored.
items.random_wrong: Freud believed conscience was inherited only from one's grandparents.
items.why: Origins and justification are separate questions; spotting the potential genetic fallacy earns evaluation credit.

items.correct: What moral terms like 'good' mean and whether moral claims can be true
items.why: It's What moral terms like 'good' mean and whether moral claims can be true. “Which charity deserves donations” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: The alleged error of defining 'good' in terms of natural properties such as pleasure
items.misconception: The mistake of believing in nature spirits
items.random_wrong: The claim that science is always wrong
items.why: Correct answer: The alleged error of defining 'good' in terms of natural properties such as pleasure. “The mistake of believing in nature spirits” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: According to emotivism, moral statements are what?
items.correct: Expressions of emotion or attitude, neither true nor false
items.why: Correct answer: Expressions of emotion or attitude, neither true nor false. “Descriptions of God's commands” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: Reason applying moral principles to particular actions
items.misconception: An infallible inner voice of God
items.why: It's Reason applying moral principles to particular actions. “An infallible inner voice of God” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
